questions.prompt: Why does broad match now beat exact match for most Smart Bidding accounts?

choices.text: Google's NLP (BERT and successors) understands query meaning, not just words — modern broad match expands semantically, not literally. Smart Bidding's per-auction logic predicts low conversion probability on borderline queries and bids near zero, effectively self-filtering bad matches. Together they cast a wider net while Smart Bidding's filter keeps cost focused on convertible queries. Tight match types restrict the learning surface and miss volume. Requires 30+ conversions/30 days for Smart Bidding to filter cleanly.

questions.explanation: NLP + Smart Bidding's per-auction filter flipped the calculus toward broad.

questions.prompt: Launching broad match on a campaign. What's the essential paired discipline?

choices.text: Build a robust negative keyword list before launch (jobs/careers, free/cheap/DIY, competitor brand names, information-only queries like 'what is', out-of-scope categories, wrong-product terms) and review the Search Terms report weekly to keep adding. Broad match without negatives is dangerous; with negatives it's safe and powerful. The pair works together — broad expands, negatives trim. Use negative phrase match type for most cases; negative broad can over-filter, negative exact under-filters.

questions.explanation: Broad match without negatives is dangerous. Build negatives first, audit weekly.
